region_of_origin: Southern China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salt-cured waxed duck. Mapped to broader duck food product class. Waxed duck is a salt-treated, dried, and steamed preparation of duck with skin.
ontology.foodex2_description: Duck meat, cured/salted — mapped under preserved poultry meat category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated waxed duck code. A00LQ (preserved poultry) is the closest defensible anchor. Documented as analogous estimate.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for waxed duck or Chinese cured duck as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 small serving as condiment or side dish
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of waxed duck is approximately 50g, often consumed as a flavouring component alongside rice or congee rather than as a main protein portion, due to its very high sodium and fat content.

nutrient_source.title: Waxed Duck — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Waxed Duck (F11040010), unspecified cut, salt treated, dried and steamed with skin. Confidence 0.95 as per original record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate for negligible-carbohydrate cured meat product
gi_source.source_match_notes: Waxed duck contains only 0.2g carbohydrate per 100g, making a formal GI measurement physiologically meaningless. No direct GI entry exists for waxed duck or Chinese cured duck in the University of Sydney GI Database. By analogy with other negligible-carbohydrate cured meat products (e.g., salted/cured pork, bacon), which carry a nominal GI of approximately 0–1 in the literature, a conservative numeric value of 1 is assigned to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric. gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 50g serving = (1 × 0.1g carb) / 100 = effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (2198mg/100g) — nearly 4× the 600mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid waxed duck or restrict intake to a very small condiment-level portion (≤20g), as a single 50g serving delivers approximately 1099mg sodium — more than half the WHO daily limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high fat content (39.9g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~13g/100g may promote insulin resistance with regular large-portion consumption, despite negligible carbohydrate (0.2g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Waxed duck poses minimal direct glycaemic impact due to negligible carbohydrate content, but the very high saturated fat load may worsen insulin resistance over time; limit to small condiment-level servings and monitor overall dietary fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of very high saturated fat (~13g/100g), extremely high sodium (2198mg/100g), and high total fat (39.9g/100g) presents a significant cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat waxed duck as an occasional flavouring condiment only (≤20g per occasion), given its high saturated fat and sodium content which together elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13g/100g), extremely high sodium (2198mg/100g), and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) generated during salt-curing and drying are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid waxed duck, as its high saturated fat, AGE content from the curing process, and excessive sodium collectively amplify joint inflammation and tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Waxed duck (腊鸭, La Ya) is a traditional Southern Chinese preserved meat product widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. It is prepared by rubbing duck — typically with skin intact — in a mixture of salt, soy sauce, Chinese rice wine (Shaoxing wine), and five-spice powder, then air-drying or sun-drying the carcass for several weeks before steaming or roasting to finish. The result is a deeply savoury, intensely flavoured, oily meat with a firm, slightly chewy texture and a rich umami-laden taste profile. Waxed duck is most commonly served as a condiment or flavouring component alongside plain rice or congee, rather than as a standalone main protein, due to its concentrated saltiness. Nutritionally, waxed duck is very high in fat (39.9g/100g), with estimated saturated fat of approximately 13g/100g, and carries an extremely high sodium load of 2198mg/100g — a direct consequence of the salt-curing preservation process. Protein content is moderate at 20.1g/100g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.2g/100g, making glycaemic impact effectively zero. Clinically, waxed duck is a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat burden. It is also a concern for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to pro-inflammatory saturated fats and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) formed during the curing and drying process. Calcium (47mg/100g) and iron (2.6mg/100g) are present in modest amounts. Waxed duck is a seasonal delicacy in Chinese culinary tradition, particularly associated with the Lunar New Year period and winter months.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in waxed duck?
Is waxed duck suitable for hypertension?
How is Chinese cured duck made?
Is waxed duck high in fat?
Can I eat waxed duck if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11040010) with a confidence of 0.95. The food identity is unambiguous. Saturated fat is estimated at ~13g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for duck with skin (closest approved analogue) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is the only estimated field.
notes: Saturated fat (13.0g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for duck meat with skin, roasted (NDB 05141), as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for waxed duck. This is the only field not directly sourced from HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for salt-cured waxed duck are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — waxed duck is a pure animal protein and fat product with no plant fibre). Glycemic index is assigned a nominal value of 1 (low category) as glycemic_index must be numeric per scoring rules; carbohydrate content is 0.2g/100g making glycaemic impact physiologically negligible. Glycemic load per 50g serving is effectively 0.0. Waxed duck is not halal-certified in standard form due to the use of Chinese rice wine in the curing marinade.
